title: Lesson 424
transcript: Privacy impact assessments. PIAs, privacy impact assessments, the whole point of these is to basically do a little bit of an assessment at the start of a new project, the implementation of a new system, to ask some questions to ask what sort of data is being collected here is any of this data personal data and if some of it is personal data where's the data being stored who's using the data who's the data being shared with how long is it being retained for You know, what different systems is this being transferred into? What laws are applicable here? Is this European Union citizen data? Is this US healthcare data and therefore HIPAA applies? So the whole point of the PIA is to essentially ask these questions. And so that's what PIAs are. Privacy Impact Assessments are a process for identifying and understanding what personal data is or is not being collected so that you can then understand the risks that might be associated with that. and then therefore identify what would be the mitigating controls to mitigate those risks. So PIAs are a very good practice and a lot of organizations conduct these. And it very much kind of falls under this idea of baking privacy in. It's just like security. We're gonna talk about this a lot throughout the course, but when you're thinking about like, when should security controls be considered and built into the system? It's not five minutes before the system goes live, right? Security should be baked into the system from the start. So should privacy. Privacy should be baked into a system from the start. And that starts with doing a PIA. So right at the very beginning of the project, asking these questions. What data is this system storing? What data is going to be used in this project? and seeing if any of it's personal data. And if it is, then you have to understand the risks associated with it and how you're going to mitigate those risks. So that's the purpose of PIAs, privacy impact assessments. And this next slide here, don't memorize this for the exam, but this gives you a good idea of the typical elements that are involved in a PIA. This is going to vary by organization, but Typically, these are the types of things you're doing. Data flow analysis, so you're understanding what data is being brought in, what systems is being transferred to, where is it being stored. Once you understand what the data is, you can see what legal implications there might be, right? If this is European Union citizen data, GDPR. If it's US health care data, HIPAA. If it's Canadian citizens' personal data, PIPEDA or PIPEDA. So different laws are going to apply depending on what personal data is being collected in what jurisdiction, how it's being used, et cetera. Which is going to lead to where you're going to want to think about what are the risks? What if this data was exposed? What if it's used inappropriately? All these sorts of things. Once you've identified the risks, you can then think about what could you do to mitigate those risks? Like maybe we just shouldn't collect this data. Or maybe we should... encrypt it with a 256-bit key, or maybe we should delete it after only 30 days. You can think about what strategies you can put in place to try and mitigate the risks. Definitely wanna document all of this, document the data flow diagrams, what legal compliance had been identified, et cetera. And as the system or the project goes through its implementation, you wanna keep up with it and make sure that the mitigating strategies, controls that have been identified have actually been implemented correctly. They're doing what they're supposed to be doing. And you want to make sure the system is being put into production correctly. And then you should do ongoing monitoring to review because things can change, right? New laws can come into effect. California not too long ago implemented a new privacy law very much modeled after GDPR. That has a big impact from a privacy perspective. You may need to update essentially your PIA for certain systems as privacy laws change, things like that. Maybe you start sharing data with a new stakeholder, he might need to kind of go back to the PIA and review things. So these are typically the steps involved in a PIA process.
